1: hallĕlû-yāh kî ṭôb zammĕrāh ʾĕlōhênû kî nāʿîm nāweh tĕhillāh — "Praise the LORD! For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and praise is fitting." Three motivation-words stack in v1: ṭôb (good, morally and aesthetically excellent), nāʿîm (pleasant, delightful — the root of the name Naomi, Ruth 1:20), and nāweh (comely, beautiful, fitting — used of a bride's beauty in Isaiah 52:7; here applied to the act of praise itself). The word-order in Hebrew places the praise-act first and then unpacks its qualities; the invitation is both aesthetic and theological: praise is fitting because it corresponds to who God is.
2: bônēh yĕrûšālayim yhwh niddĕḥê yiśrāʾēl yĕkannēs — "the LORD builds up Jerusalem; he gathers together the outcasts of Israel." The active participle bônēh (building, Qal participle from bānāh) presents the act as ongoing, not completed — YHWH is the perpetual builder of Zion. niddĕḥê (the outcasts/scattered ones, Niphal participle of nādaḥ, to drive away, banish) — the participle carries the social reality of exile: people forcibly pushed to the margins. yĕkannēs (gathers together, Piel imperfect of kānaś) — the Piel intensive marks the thoroughness of the gathering; what was dispersed is completely assembled. Post-exilic Jerusalem reads this as the return from Babylon (Isaiah 56:8; Ezekiel 34:13).
3: hārōpēʾ lišbûrê lēb ûmĕḥabbēš lĕʿaṣṣĕbôtām — "he heals the brokenhearted and bandages their wounds." Two paired verbs: rāpāʾ (heal, Qal participle — the root of Raphael and rĕpāʾîm; the medical metaphor of divine therapy) and ḥābāš (bind up/bandage wounds, Piel participle — the verb used for binding a wound in Isaiah 1:6 and for YHWH binding the broken of Isaiah 61:1). šibūrê lēb (brokenhearted, literally "broken of heart" — šābar in Niphal participle + lēb) — the same construction appears in Isaiah 61:1, the Servant's commission. The juxtaposition of v2 (gathering scattered exiles) with v3 (healing inner wounds) is deliberate: restoration is both political and personal.
4: môneh mispār lakkôkābîm lĕkullām šēmôt yiqrāʾ — "he counts the stars by number; he calls them all by name." mānāh (counts, Qal participle — used for census-taking and rationing; the root of mĕnāʾēl, God has numbered) + mispār (number, count) — the combination emphasizes enumeration. The tension with v4b is deliberate: the stars are innumerable (Genesis 15:5, where YHWH challenged Abraham to count them), yet YHWH assigns each one a number. lĕkullām šēmôt yiqrāʾ (to all of them he calls names) — naming as an act of sovereignty and intimate knowledge; in ANE thought, to name something was to define its nature and own it (Genesis 1:5, 8, 10; Psalms 2:19–20). The God who knows the name of every star also knows the wound of every broken heart (v3) — the psalm's central theological paradox: cosmic omniscience and pastoral intimacy are the same attribute.
5: gādôl ʾădōnênû wĕrabbāh kōaḥ lîtĕbûnātô ʾên mispār — "great is our Lord and abundant in power; his understanding has no number/limit." gādôl (great) + rabbāh kōaḥ (abundant/great power) are familiar divine epithets, but the verse's force is in the final phrase: lîtĕbûnātô ʾên mispār — "of his understanding there is no number." tĕbûnāh (understanding, discernment, from bîn) is the faculty of deep insight — the same noun as in Proverbs 3:19 ("by understanding he established the heavens"). The echo of mispār (number) from v4 is the key: YHWH counts the stars' number (mispār), yet his own understanding is without number (ʾên mispār). The counter-who-cannot-be-counted.
6: mĕʿôdēd ʿănāwîm yhwh mašpîl rĕšāʿîm ʿădê-āreṣ — "the LORD lifts up the humble; he brings the wicked down to the ground." ʿōdēd (lifts up, sustains — Piel participle from ʿûd, to support, restore, refresh; rare form) — the sustained support of those who are bent down. ʿănāwîm (the humble/afflicted/poor — from ʿānāh, to be bowed down, afflicted; the class of those who are socially powerless and have only YHWH as their resource — same word as Psalms 37:11 "the meek will inherit the earth" and Numbers 12:3 "Moses was very meek"). mašpîl rĕšāʿîm ʿădê-āreṣ (casts the wicked to the earth/ground, Hiphil of šāpal) — the reversal pattern that runs through Hannah's prayer (1 Samuel 2:7–8), the Magnificat (Luke 1:52), and the beatitudes.
7: ʿănû layhwh bĕtôdāh zammĕrû lēʾlōhênû bĕkinnôr — "sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make music to our God with the lyre." The shift from description (vv 1–6) to imperative command marks a new movement. ʿānāh (respond, answer in song — Qal imperative; the verb used in the antiphonal singing of Exodus 15:21 and 1 Samuel 18:7) with tôdāh (thanksgiving, the thank-offering — from yādāh, to confess/praise; tôdāh refers both to the spoken thanks and the sacrificial offering that accompanies it, Leviticus 7:12). zāmar (make music on a stringed instrument — Piel imperative, the root of mizmôr = psalm; consistently used for instrumental music as distinct from vocal praise). kinnôr (lyre/harp — the instrument of David and the Levitical choir).
8: hamĕkassēh šāmayim bĕʿābîm hammēkîn lāʾāreṣ māṭār hammāṣmîaḥ hārîm ḥāṣîr — "he covers the sky with clouds, prepares rain for the earth, makes grass grow on the hills." Three Hiphil participles in sequence, each presenting YHWH as the ongoing agent of natural provision: kāsāh (covers, Piel), kûn (prepares/establishes, Hiphil), ṣāmaḥ (causes to grow, Hiphil). The sequence — clouds → rain → grass — traces the supply chain of agricultural life. The theological force is anti-Baʿal polemic: the Canaanite storm-god Baʿal was credited with sending rain; the psalm insists it is YHWH who prepares (actively, purposefully) rain for the earth. ḥāṣîr (grass/hay/green vegetation) on the mountains points to vv 8–9's chain: vegetation feeds animals; YHWH is the fountainhead of the entire food system.
9: nôtēn libhēmāh laḥmāh libĕnê ʿōrēb ʾăšer yiqrāʾû — "he gives to the beast its food, and to the young ravens when they call." bĕhēmāh (beast — large quadruped, domestic or wild; the general animal class) — YHWH provides food for animals in general before the ravens are specified. bĕnê ʿōrēb (sons of the raven, young ravens) — ravens were classified as unclean birds under Levitical law (Leviticus 11:15; Deuteronomy 14:14) and yet YHWH feeds their young. ʾăšer yiqrāʾû (who call/cry out) — the ravens' cries are presented as a form of petition that YHWH hears and answers; Job 38:41 uses the same image ("who provides for the raven its prey, when its young ones cry to God?"). Jesus echoes this in Luke 12:24 ("consider the ravens; they do not sow or reap... and yet God feeds them").
10: lōʾ bigĕbûrat hassûs yĕḥpāṣ lōʾ bĕšôqê hāʾîš yirṣeh — "he does not delight in the strength of the horse, nor take pleasure in the legs of a man." The verse is structured as a double negative: lōʾ... yĕḥpāṣ (does not delight in) / lōʾ... yirṣeh (does not take pleasure in). gĕbûrat hassûs (strength/might of the horse) — in the ANE, the horse was the decisive weapon of war; Egyptian and Assyrian armies were feared for their cavalry. šôqê hāʾîš (legs/thighs of a man) — the legs of an infantryman, his physical power. Both are military metaphors: YHWH is not impressed by conventional military strength (Psalms 20:7; Isaiah 31:1; Deuteronomy 17:16 — the king must not multiply horses). Human military power is irrelevant to divine favor.
11: rôṣeh yhwh ʾet-yĕrēʾāyw ʾet-hamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô — "the LORD takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love." The contrast with v10 is sharp: YHWH's rāṣāh (pleasure, favorable regard) is not given to military power but to a spiritual disposition. yĕrēʾāyw (those who fear him — Qal participle plural of yārēʾ; the fundamental posture of covenantal reverence that combines awe, obedience, and trust). hamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô (those who hope/wait in his steadfast love) — yāḥal (to wait with expectant hope, Piel participle) + ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love — the central Hebrew theological term for YHWH's faithful, committed love within the covenant relationship). The combination is the Psalter's definition of godly life: reverential fear of YHWH coupled with hopeful waiting on his covenant faithfulness.
12: šabbĕḥî yĕrûšālayim ʾet-yhwh hallĕlî ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn — "praise the LORD, O Jerusalem! Praise your God, O Zion!" The second half of the psalm (vv 12–20) begins with a direct address to Jerusalem/Zion — a shift from the general praise of vv 1–11. šābaḥ (praise, commend, laud — Piel imperative; slightly distinct from hālal; šābaḥ tends toward public proclamation and commendation of another's excellence). The personification of Jerusalem as a praising subject is characteristic of the Songs of Zion (Ps 48; 87; Isaiah 40:9; Psalms 52:1). The LXX counts this as the beginning of a new psalm (LXX Psalm 147), suggesting early Jewish recognition of its distinct character.
13: kî ḥizzaq bĕrîḥê šĕʿārayik bērēk bānayik bĕqirbēk — "for he has reinforced the bars of your gates and blessed your children within you." ḥizzaq (reinforced/strengthened — Piel perfect of ḥāzaq) the bĕrîḥîm (crossbars/locking bars) of the šĕʿārîm (gates) — the practical image of Nehemiah's rebuilding: the gate-bars that lock the city against siege or attack (Nehemiah 3:3, 6, 13; Psalms 7:3). The bars of the gates represent the entire defensive infrastructure of restored Jerusalem. The verse pairs external security (bars of gates) with internal blessing (children within) — the city restored to both protection and fertility.
14: haśśām-gĕbûlēk šālôm ḥēleb ḥiṭṭîm yaśbîʿēk — "he makes peace your border, and satisfies you with the finest wheat." šālôm as the content of borders — the territory under YHWH's covenant protection is not defined merely by geography but by wholeness and well-being. ḥēleb ḥiṭṭîm (finest/fat of wheat) — ḥēleb (fat, the richest portion; same word as the fat reserved for sacrifice in Leviticus 3:16) applied to wheat = the choicest grain. The fat of wheat as temple provision (Deuteronomy 32:14 refers to Israel being fed "the finest of wheat"; Psalms 81:16) — the restored community receives sacrificial-quality provision for ordinary sustenance.
15: haššōlēaḥ ʾimrātô āreṣ ʿad-mĕhērāh yārûṣ dĕbārô — "he sends his command/word to the earth; his word runs swiftly." ʾimrāh (word/command — from ʾāmar, to say; the specific form that emphasizes authoritative speech) + dābār (word, matter, thing — the comprehensive divine utterance) appear together to emphasize the complete authority of YHWH's speech. yārûṣ (runs, Qal imperfect of rûṣ) — the personification of the divine word as a swift messenger racing across the earth. Vv 15–18 then describe the divine word's work in nature: it commands snow, frost, hail, cold, and then the thaw. This sequence creates the theological context for v19: the same word that commands weather is the word that commands Israel — Torah and cosmos share one divine speech-act.
16: hannōtēn šeleg kaṣṣāmer kĕpôr kāʾēper yĕpazzēr — "he gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes." Two similes: šeleg (snow) compared to ṣemer (wool) — the white, fluffy covering of both; and kĕpôr (hoarfrost, frost crystals) compared to ʾēper (ash/ashes) — the grayish, powdery scatter of both. yĕpazzēr (scatters, Piel imperfect of pāzar — to scatter, spread abroad; the Piel suggests thoroughness of the scattering). The poetic genius is in the domestic images: wool (from the flock) and ash (from the hearth) make cosmic phenomena familiar; YHWH's winter provision is as natural as household goods.
17: mašlîk qārāḥô kĕpittîm lipnê qārātô mî yaʿămōd — "he hurls down his ice like morsels/crumbs; who can stand before his cold?" qeraḥ (ice, hailstones — solid frozen water; can refer to crystal-clear ice from its appearance) + pittîm (morsels, pieces, crumbs of bread — from pat, a morsel) — hailstones scattered like bread crumbs; the image is both vivid (size and quantity) and carries a trace of divine playfulness. mî yaʿămōd (who can stand/withstand?) — a rhetorical question expecting the answer "no one"; the divine cold is an overwhelming force. The question echoes Nahum 1:6 ("who can stand before his indignation?") and Malachi 3:2 ("who can endure the day of his coming?") — the divine power in nature foreshadows eschatological power.
18: yišlaḥ dĕbārô wĕyamsēm yašsēb rûḥô yizzĕlû-māyim — "he sends his word and melts them; he makes his wind blow and the waters flow." The structural echo is precise: v15 sends (šālaḥ) his word to earth; here v18 sends (šālaḥ) his word to melt the ice. The same divine speech-act that produced the cold (vv 16–17) now dissolves it. wĕyamsēm (melts them, Hiphil — causative of māsas, to melt, dissolve; the subject is the divine word melting the snow and frost). yašsēb rûḥô (he makes his wind blow — causes to blow, Hiphil of nāšab) — the warm wind (the sirocco/east wind that dries and thaws in the Levant); the same rûaḥ (wind/spirit) that swept over the waters at creation (Genesis 1:2). yizzĕlû-māyim (the waters flow — Qal imperfect, the resulting thaw and runoff).
19: maggid dĕbārô lĕyaʿăqōb ḥuqqāyw ûmišpāṭāyw lĕyiśrāʾēl — "he declares his word to Jacob, his statutes and ordinances to Israel." The transition from cosmic dābār (vv 15–18) to covenantal dābār is the theological climax. maggid (declares/makes known — Hiphil participle of nāgad, to tell, report, declare) emphasizes the communicative act: YHWH does not merely possess his word but speaks it to Jacob. ḥuqqāyw (his statutes — from ḥāqaq, to engrave, decree; specific covenant terms) + mišpāṭāyw (his judgments/ordinances — the case-law of covenant governance). yiśrāʾēl (Israel) + yaʿăqōb (Jacob) — the covenant double-name; the two names together indicate the whole people under both their ancestral name (Jacob) and their covenant-struggle name (Israel).
20: lōʾ-ʿāśāh kēn lĕkol-gôy ûmišpāṭîm bal-yĕdāʿûm hallĕlû-yāh — "he has not done this for any other nation; they do not know his ordinances. Praise the LORD!" The exclusivity of the covenant-gift is stated plainly and without apology: lōʾ-ʿāśāh kēn (he has not done so) lĕkol-gôy (for any nation — the word gôy, nation, is used for non-Israelite peoples; here it is all-inclusive: no nation has received what Israel received). ûmišpāṭîm bal-yĕdāʿûm (they do not know his judgments — bal is the emphatic negation; the nations have simply not been given the Torah). This is a statement of historical election, not an assertion of the nations' moral worthlessness; Paul takes up the same theme in Romans 9:4–5 and Psalms 3:1–2 ("what advantage has the Jew? Much in every way; first, they were entrusted with the oracles of God"). hallĕlû-yāh — the doxological close.
